title: Ivanhoe
description: Scott's historical romance of chivalry, nationhood, and conflict after the Norman conquest.
descriptionParagraphs: Scott's historical romance of chivalry, nationhood, and conflict after the Norman conquest.

Ivanhoe is usually read through its treatment of history, nationhood, and Jewish representation. As a novel, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes history, nationhood, and Jewish representation feel immediate.

overview.englishDescription: Scott's historical romance of chivalry, nationhood, and conflict after the Norman conquest.
banningParagraphs: Ivanhoe entered censorship debates as a novel associated with history, nationhood, and Jewish representation. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around antisemitism and ideological control.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1930s in Germany, where Nazi authorities banned circulation. The book was suppressed for featuring Jewish characters sympathetically. This is a stark example of a regime banning even old canonical fiction on racial grounds.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: The Third Reich in Power, 1933-1939
counterReadings.author: Richard J. Evans
counterReadings.note: A historical counterweight to fascist mythmaking and to books used as authoritarian propaganda.

counterReadings.title: Introduction to the Holocaust
counterReadings.author: United States Holocaust Memorial Museum
counterReadings.note: A factual public rebuttal resource for antisemitic and Holocaust-distorting literature.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
